[hide=Transparency Report]
[list][*][b]Accidentally sent MrVancoover’s role PM to NoSignOfTheta. 
[/list]

Both players were VTs and the error resulted because of an error in copy/pasting role PMs. All PMs were prewritten, but for some reason, the wrong role PM was copy/pasted when role PMs were sent out. As the error was caught relatively early in the game, the decision was made to completely rerand the game. 

[list][*][b]A reminder for the mafia factional kill was not sent N1.[/list]

The provision added in OCRSTM v. 4.1.1 guaranteeing a reminder to submit night actions halfway through the night was removed in OCRSTM v. 7.0. As a result, when the mafia factional kill was not submitted, a reminder was sent extremely late, resulting in the mafia not performing a factional kill. There was a potential oversight as one mafia member was hanged, while the other was on V/LA through EOD1 and EON1, however we do not believe this affected the outcome of the game as the late-submitted kill would have been blocked by the doctor. The night action reminder will be added back in the upcoming OCRSTM 9. 

[list][*][b]The Tracker result was not sent on time.[/list]

The N1 Track result of “No movement” was originally not sent until the Tracker asked about the result. The error was a simple oversight because the result was not a positive, although the Tracker should have received a response. The error may possibly have contributed to the Tracker mistakenly thinking that they were roleblocked, leading them to claim, and we apologize. However, it should be noted that a roleblocker cannot exist in a tracker setup. 
[/hide]

post_rendered:
General feedback and analysis on the game

Day 1: iC a disaster modTM

We’re ignoring everything before Post #36 because that was a different rand. Technically I guess there could be some angleshooting where you try to read people before/after the rerand, but I don’t think anyone would’ve noticed anything significant even if they tried because of just how early and short it was.

On the basics of scumreading/townreading
What BobDBuilder321 posted at #57 and after is roughly correct. The most basic fundamentals of playing this game are
  • Actively asking yourself why people did or posted something, and whether it makes them townier, scummier, or is just Non Alignment Indicative (NAI).
  • Not just scumhunting, but finding good reasons to townread people too. This can be very useful in narrowing down who could be scum through Process of Elimination (PoE)!
  • Keeping in mind win conditions. These are listed in the PMs, but it usually can be interpreted as the following. For VTs: find scum, be towny so people townread you, or both. For Town PRs: find scum, don’t get hanged, stay hidden until you can do your job (or unless you’re really gonna die), or all of the above. For Mafia: be towny, don’t die, find Town PRs and kill them, or all of the above.
  • Using these winconditions to infer what each alignment would do in a certain situation and compare that to what people are actually doing! Called “motivation reads,” this is basically determining whether a player is town or scum by seeing if they’re doing what town’s supposed to do, or what scum’s supposed to do. For example, scum wants to hang lots of people without discussing too much, so someone who votes without thinking or saying much is more likely to be scum!


On Bob’s fake/real scumslip, interactions comment, and what he did right
On post #79:
BobDBuilder321 wrote:
mj my quicktopic isn't working uh
I’m not sure whether it was ever stated, but this is very NAI.
Scum!Bob (and 99% of other non-newbie players) never say this accidentally. Town!Bob never says it accidentally either. Therefore, it’s a very intentional “slip” for whatever unknown reason. You could try to read him over that (and in general over jokes like “I’m mafia!”), but I wouldn’t recommend it because it’s more or less a coinflip unless you know that person inside and out.

On post #83:
BobDBuilder321 wrote:
Still wondering why exactly Rohan said that, but I think I can safely call that interaction not w/w.
This is highly questionable. The concept of “w/w” or “v/v” means a pair of players are unlikely to be both scum (wolves) or town (villagers) for some reason. The core assumption that Bob makes here is that mafia would want to distance from each other and not appear too friendly. While this is a valid assumption, saying “hi!” is not enough to justify calling two players “not w/w,” ever.

The thing that Bob did correctly which you should consider doing yourself was being proactive early game. It’s very easy to fall into the mindset of, “Nothing’s happened yet so I’m just not going to say anything,” but this is not necessarily correct. Most of the time, it’s better to make a questionable and half-baked read to gauge people’s reactions or prod them rather than not doing anything. Complacency and apathy are one of the top things that are best to avoid in general.


About ISOs, opportunism, and the best time to hang
Comments on ISOs and PBPAs: ISOs are something that people do that’s too often ends up being quantity over quality. An ISO is usually isolating someone’s posts and looking at just them (instead of the thread as a whole) to judge their scumminess or towniness. A Post By Post Analysis (PBPA) is the most common form of an ISO post, where someone quote’s all or most of their target’s posts and comments on them one by one. It’s long, it looks intimidating and fancy, and it takes a lot of time to do. It’s also an inefficient use of your time in most cases, because PBPAs often end up vague and inconclusive. Try other methods of scumreading before resorting to a PBPA!

On opportunism: regarding post #271, opportunism is when someone votes (or tries to hang) a player who seems like they’d be an easy mishang. Also called low-hanging fruit (LHF), these players are often inactive, post weirdly, or have trouble articulating their thoughts. If you see a player voting these LHF for bad reasons, or not seeming to care about whether they’re just scummy town, then this could be scum-indicative. Town wants LHF to be sorted into either town or scum and should try to read them, while scum just wants an easy hang. That’s theoretically, anyways. In practice, it doesn’t always work out, so beware.
fifty51 wrote:
when is the best time to hang? would we wait until deadline or do we start voting like 3 days prior for roleclaim
Usually, it’s better for town to take advantage of the discussion time that they have and hang close to deadline; more discussion is generally better for town while mafia prefer faster hangs with less discussion. However, people tend to get impatient, which can lead to rushed hangs, in which case you’ll have to think about whether it was just overzealous town or if the hang was really driven by mafia. There are some cases where you might consider hanging earlier, however. For example, if only majority hang is on and you need a significant number of players to vote (like more than 10), then it’s a good idea to try to gather votes starting from a week before EOD in case people don’t show up and you can’t hang anyone.


About the mafia’s fake reads and “there’s always one scum in the experienced players”
On the scumcases/reads the scum members have posted:
  1. Bob’s scumcase on Rohan in post #429: it’s a really poo take. It’s very much NAI. Maybe there’s an argument to be made that Rohan was the scummiest at that moment, but the argument itself in a vacuum was very lacking and should never be the main reason why you hang someone, especially after D1.
  2. ben’s first post in post #438: it’s also questionable, and I don’t recommend using them as general scumtells. “Competent newbie syndrome” or the competent newbie lemma is highly speculative and it’s doubtful if it works accurately. Self consciousness is also often debated over whether it’s actually scummy or not, and it’s usually said to be NAI unless it’s paired with something like meta.

On there statistically being one scum in the SE/ICs: stuff like post #440 is probably less serious. Some people say that there’s always at least one experienced scum. This isn’t something that’s guaranteed in RNG: roles are always randomized, and the mods do not choose who gets what role or alignment. So you can treat statements like this as gutreads pretty much, because it stems from the belief that a entirely newbie scumteam would have a hard time winning or playing the game. This has been proven false, statistically speaking: all-newbie scumteams have won before.

The one thing you should keep in mind, alluded to briefly in this post, is the halfway rule. Generally, scum will try to kill experienced players first because they have a higher chance of finding scum. The halfway rule means that if an experienced player, especially a player with a reputation of being killed off early, is still alive by D3/D4, you should scrutinize whether or not they’re just playing really well as scum. How applicable this is as a hard rule (ie. always hang them if they’re alive by D4) is debatable, but it’s still something to keep in mind.


About that blatant admission of PLing, about bandwagons, and about claims
By the way, these two posts:
fdas wrote:
Unvote

I don't think there is much of a case for pursuing a hang on this slot now that snoozing is gone.
benjaminsz wrote:
Unvote in the sense that I'm not going to vote for ostriches for what snoozing did but because snoozing was scummy ostriches is in my SL pool
You should know that fdas and benjaminsz are basically admitting that their pushes on snoozing were policy hangs (PLs), basically hanging someone because you don’t like them. Technically, you’re not supposed to do this. Also technically, although this could be modbias, look at the way benjaminsz talks about his vote/unvote here. It’s very awkward: he’s defending his SR on snoozing over snoozing not trying, but also unvoting, but also leaving open the possibility of voting ostriches again if other people start SRing ostriches too. It doesn’t really look like he cares about what ostriches’s alignment is, and interested only in whether he can hang him or not. (This also applies to fdas.) Posts like this are stuff that should make you pay closer attention, because there’s potentially Alignment Indicative (AI) motivations here.

About bandwagoning: in post #484, ben states the reason for his SR on math202 is. It’s not a bad SR, but let’s go over the hows and whys. Following what other people say or vote, called “bandwagoning” or “sheeping” is considered some combination of bad or scummy. This is because when people only follow what other people do, they don’t post their opinions (even if they have them). This makes them harder to read, which is bad for town, because town needs to sort people into town or scum. Conversely, it’s good for scum because they like it when it’s harder to tell whether someone’s town or scum. This makes having and posting your own thoughts important, even if you do actually agree with someone. That said, it doesn’t mean that bandwagoning or sheeping is illegal. You’re probably just gonna get scumread for it if that’s the only thing you’re doing.

On claims:
ostriches88 wrote:
mmmmmmmmmm

ok fine

i am doctor

pls dont hang meee

hang math gogogo
In another world, we talk about evaluating power role (PR) claims, but this is not that world. Just know that the default (so basically always unless you have a really good reason) is to accept PR claims at face value. This is for two reasons: a) PR claims on AoPS are usually actual claims, outside of a handful of players who like to lie about their roles, and b) a lot of the time, the PR who claimed will die overnight. So they will be “resolved” overnight, and there’s no reason to do the mafia’s job for them and hang them early. If you want to get fancy, you can work out whether or not it’s optimal for mafia to claim as certain PRs, but that’s complicated and don’t worry about it for now because it doesn’t happen that often.

In another world, one could argue that fdas asking 1SBP to counterclaim (cc) ostriches is suboptimal, but this is still not that world, so don’t worry about that either. It was probably the correct thing to do.


And that’s it, cuz I don’t think there’s anything to comment on for night actions or D2. There’s still a lot of stuff uh stuffed into the hide tags, so have fun. Feedback wasn’t that late, was it? Refer to individual feedback for more detailed stuff, and thanks for playing!

Best,
mathjay

post_rendered:
Individual player feedback

1. fdas
From mathjay:

My greatest regret is messing up the original rand. ;-;

2.1 BobDBuilder321
From mathjay:

XD

2.2 benjaminsz
From mathjay:

Unfortunate. I apologize for the late NK reminder, but conceding was perhaps not the move. I’m sorry if the game stressed you out though!

3.1 snoozingnewt
From mathjay:
snoozingnewt wrote:
But anyway you're going to make this fat confbiased ISO, tunnel me, and then ruin the game so like
When did you turn into mark by the way

3.2 ostriches88
From mathjay:
ostriches88 wrote:
i would like to hang ben/fdas/math/fifty/maybe rose today
i think there is 1 scum in {math, fifty}, and one in {ben, fdas}. so idk who we hang first. ben probably before fdas and math before fifty. so i would like to hang ben/math today, and if ben town, hang fdas. otherwise, hang math

ig it is possible that ben/fdas w/w, so if ben scum, and math town, hang fdas.
This is an extremely ambitious hangpool. It’s also missing explanations or reasoning, which aren’t mandatory but - and I’m sure you know this, but - explaining reads are usually better. Even if you already think you’re towny enough or you’re convinced you’re right, someone might point out a pretty big error in reasoning you might’ve made. fifty51 wrote:

This is a frustrating problem that you’ll often bump into! The key thing to “fix” this problem is proactivity and engaging with people. First, try to ask them questions. Ask them for their reads. Or if you think they’re scummy, you could try pressuring them. Vote them, make a case against them, and see how they respond. If you see them in the thread, try to have a conversation with them to gauge their thought process.

Of course, this doesn’t always work and some people will just be inactive. In that case, the only thing you can do is just do the best with what you have. Two important tools that are useful are Process of Elimination and interactions read. With PoE, you can narrow down the people who you think are scum to a smaller list that you can pay special attention to; it’s easier than straight up trying to scumread sometimes. Then add interaction analysis on top of that: these players seem particularly friendly. Are they scum? Or this player’s trying to stop another player’s wagon; how would they look if that wagon flips scum? Interactions become powerful once you have confirmed flips, and you can use it to fill in the gaps. Even if someone isn’t posting, maybe they have suspicious interactions with someone who just flipped scum!

Your thoughts in post #412 were pretty solid. Pressuring or looking closer at players who aren’t contributing as much is something you should do in pretty much all games. Your reply in #516 to ostriches was good as well. You listed a couple of “not w/w” pairs in #530, which was really good! You didn’t do this, but just so you know, pre-flip cases or interactions reads can go horribly wrong, which is why you generally don’t rely on interaction reads without a flip unless it’s a “not w/w” read, like you did. Just one thing:
fifty51 wrote:
Rayford too, since it would give 0 info.
Usually avoid hanging for info. The info is not worth it a lot of the time and people don’t use said “info” after pushing for an info hang anyways. It’s also often used to justify lazy hangs.

This was a really nice call though. Good job!

Overall, you did really well for your first game. Just remember that there are ways to work around reading inactive players, using POE, and proactively pressuring or interacting with other players. You did great, though, so don’t worry about it too much. I hope you had fun, and good luck in your future games!

5. MrVancoover
From mathjay:

Post #90 was a really great early post! The sort of thought process you have there is a very good thing to have in mafia. You also knowingly or unknowingly used the concept of “NAI,” namely that not everything has to be scummy or towny. Some things are just personal quirks, or too difficult to tell, and that’s fine sometimes.

You were also partly right that defensiveness is not the greatest of scumtells. Town become overly defensive after getting voted or scumread all the time, and more so with newer/less experienced players. Usually, defensiveness only becomes scummy when someone’s only defending and not doing anything else, but even that’s not bulletproof. You’ll eventually get a feel for it! For now, it’s something to think about when you ever bump into this in the future (and you will).
MrVancoover wrote:
I cannot make any soundproof assumptions without reasonable analytical proof (and the proof that can be gathered right now is not exactly the most strenuous.)

However, I think that theta is tl and rohan is null/slight sl.
snoozing commented on this in post #138, but small food for thought: there is little definite proof in mafia outside of really glaring things like cop checks or scumslips. Mafia is still a guessing game, although the way we play it makes it a highly analytical one. This implies two things: there’s always a way to convince someone you’re not scum if you try hard enough, and there’s always a chance your most foolproof airtight case comes to the wrong conclusion.

Overall, you did pretty well for your first game! The things I would work on is branching out more, interacting with people more, and making more reads. Here are some tips if you’re having trouble making reads!
The most basic fundamentals to forming reads are:
  • Actively asking yourself why people did or posted something, and whether it makes them townier, scummier, or is just Non Alignment Indicative (NAI).
  • Not just scumhunting, but finding good reasons to townread people too. This can be very useful in narrowing down who could be scum through Process of Elimination (PoE)!
  • Keeping in mind win conditions. These are listed in the PMs, but it usually can be interpreted as the following. For VTs: find scum, be towny so people townread you, or both. For Town PRs: find scum, don’t get hanged, stay hidden until you can do your job (or unless you’re really gonna die), or all of the above. For Mafia: be towny, don’t die, find Town PRs and kill them, or all of the above.
  • Using these winconditions to infer what each alignment would do in a certain situation and compare that to what people are actually doing! Called “motivation reads,” this is basically determining whether a player is town or scum by seeing if they’re doing what town’s supposed to do, or what scum’s supposed to do. For example, scum wants to hang lots of people without discussing too much, so someone who votes without thinking or saying much is more likely to be scum!
But even outside of reads, people find it often to post their thoughts and just comment on what’s happening in the thread; it helps you clarify your thoughts, and get townread by other people too! Just something to think about for next time. I hope you had fun, and good luck in your future games!

Don’t worry about being voted over small things like this. Usually, the people who are more sensibly inclined will protect you! It’s still good to be aware of the rules and what’s going on in the thread once you’ve joined a mafia game though.

If you’re having trouble figuring out what to post (as town or mafia), here are some quick tips!
  1. Don’t feel scared! It might feel like you can’t make any mistakes or you’ll die, but your “mistakes” aren’t actually noticed by other people a lot of the time. So just post what you think, especially in Day 1, and work from there!
  2. Make sure to explain your thought process. It’s okay to be wrong, but if you talk about why you thought x y z, it’ll help you develop better reads and help other people develop reads on you!
  3. If you find yourself stuck, find a post that stood out to you (in a good or bad way!) and try to think through the motivations behind it. Would town say this? What about scum? What would scum have to gain from this post? Town? Neither? This is the basic building blocks a lot of reads are made out of!

Otherwise, it was a pretty good effort for your first game! Randing mafia in your first game is kind of rough, and it was unlucky that your partner was inactive during EOD. It’ll become easier as you play more games though, so don’t worry about it too much and try to have fun. Good luck in your future games!

This is called a “tinfoil.” It’s when you’re paranoid that a player who is active, talking a lot, and generally towny is secretly just a really good mafia. This is a powerful read in the right situations. However, you should be careful not to let your paranoia sabotage your townreads for no reason; someone could just be genuinely trying their best as town. Generally, if you want to make a tinfoil read and say that you have one, make sure to have at least one really good reason! For example, here, you could say that Bob was spamming the thread (like is ISO) but not very constructively, which would make your tinfoil a lot more solid!

Overall, you did really well for your first game. Just remember that there are ways to work around reading inactive players, using POE, and proactively pressuring or interacting with other players. You did great, though, so don’t worry about it too much. I hope you had fun, and good luck in your future games! 
[/hide]
[hide=5. MrVancoover]
[b]From mathjay:[/b]

[url=https://aops.com/community/p22661223]Post #90[/url] was a really great early post! The sort of thought process you have there is a very good thing to have in mafia. You also knowingly or unknowingly used the concept of “NAI,” namely that not everything has to be scummy or towny. Some things are just personal quirks, or too difficult to tell, and that’s fine sometimes. 

You were also partly right that defensiveness is not the greatest of scumtells. Town become overly defensive after getting voted or scumread all the time, and more so with newer/less experienced players. Usually, defensiveness only becomes scummy when someone’s only defending and not doing anything else, but even that’s not bulletproof. You’ll eventually get a feel for it! For now, it’s something to think about when you ever bump into this in the future (and you will). 
[quote name="MrVancoover" url="/community/p22663632"]
I cannot make any soundproof assumptions without reasonable analytical proof (and the proof that can be gathered right now is not exactly the most strenuous.)

However, I think that theta is tl and rohan is null/slight sl.
[/quote]
snoozing commented on this in [url=https://aops.com/community/p22666447]post #138[/url], but small food for thought: there is little definite proof in mafia outside of really glaring things like cop checks or scumslips. Mafia is still a guessing game, although the way we play it makes it a highly analytical one. This implies two things: there’s always a way to convince someone you’re not scum if you try hard enough, and there’s always a chance your most foolproof airtight case comes to the wrong conclusion. 

Overall, you did pretty well for your first game! The things I would work on is branching out more, interacting with people more, and making more reads. [hide=Here are some tips if you’re having trouble making reads!]The most basic fundamentals to forming reads are:
[list][*]Actively asking yourself why people did or posted something, and whether it makes them townier, scummier, or is just Non Alignment Indicative (NAI).
[*]Not just scumhunting, but finding good reasons to townread people too. This can be very useful in narrowing down who could be scum through Process of Elimination (PoE)!
[*]Keeping in mind win conditions. These are listed in the PMs, but it usually can be interpreted as the following. For VTs: find scum, be towny so people townread you, or both. For Town PRs: find scum, don’t get hanged, stay hidden until you can do your job (or unless you’re really gonna die), or all of the above. For Mafia: be towny, don’t die, find Town PRs and kill them, or all of the above. 
[*]Using these winconditions to infer what each alignment would do in a certain situation and compare that to what people are actually doing! Called “motivation reads,” this is basically determining whether a player is town or scum by seeing if they’re doing what town’s supposed to do, or what scum’s supposed to do. For example, scum wants to hang lots of people without discussing too much, so someone who votes without thinking or saying much is more likely to be scum! [/list][/hide] But even outside of reads, people find it often to post their thoughts and just comment on what’s happening in the thread; it helps you clarify your thoughts, and get townread by other people too! Just something to think about for next time. I hope you had fun, and good luck in your future games! 
[/hide]
[hide=6. NoSignOfTheta]
[b]From mathjay:[/b]

You got questioned over your Rohan TL a lot, but posts like #147: 
[quote name="NoSignOfTheta" url="/community/p22666978"]
i mean rohan is tl because of his defensive mode. not tr because thats not enough, but not null either because he did something towny.

are great! It’s okay to be confused/unsure over your reads D1. The important part is trying to articulate yourself and explain what reads you have, even if you think your reads aren’t great. This could a) get you help in improving your reads through talking with people, and b) help people to hopefully townread you because they now know your thoughts a bit better!

Although everyone criticized you for townreading defensiveness, it’s not that wrong. If it makes you feel that it’s a towny kind of defensiveness instead of a scummy kind of defensiveness, that’s a valid read. People won’t necessarily agree with you unless you come up with a convincing reason, but that gutreads exist and are perfectly fine. 

Don’t worry too much if people end up scumreading you! It feels horrible and stressful sometimes, but often the best thing you can do is shrug it off, respond adequately if you need to, and keep scumhunting and contributing. If town is good enough, they’ll eventually realize that you’re actually towny!

Whether townreads or scumreads, having some kind of reads are necessary!

Otherwise, it was pretty decent for your first game! You did great even though you were pressured, and I think you’ll have a much easier time once you start playing more games and continue what you’re doing. Don’t worry about being voted over small things like this. Usually, the people who are more sensibly inclined will protect you! It’s still good to be aware of the rules and what’s going on in the thread once you’ve joined a mafia game though. 

If you’re having trouble figuring out what to post (as town or mafia), here are some quick tips! 
[list=1][*]Don’t feel scared! It might feel like you can’t make any mistakes or you’ll die, but your “mistakes” aren’t actually noticed by other people a lot of the time. So just post what you think, especially in Day 1, and work from there!
[*]Make sure to explain your thought process. It’s okay to be wrong, but if you talk about why you thought x y z, it’ll help you develop better reads and help other people develop reads on you!
[*]If you find yourself stuck, find a post that stood out to you (in a good or bad way!) and try to think through the motivations behind it. Would town say this? What about scum? What would scum have to gain from this post? Town? Neither? This is the basic building blocks a lot of reads are made out of! [/list]

Otherwise, it was a pretty good effort for your first game! Randing mafia in your first game is kind of rough, and it was unlucky that your partner was inactive during EOD. It’ll become easier as you play more games though, so don’t worry about it too much and try to have fun. Good luck in your future games!

This is called a “tinfoil.” It’s when you’re paranoid that a player who is active, talking a lot, and generally towny is secretly just a really good mafia. This is a powerful read in the right situations. However, you should be careful not to let your paranoia sabotage your townreads for no reason; someone could just be genuinely trying their best as town. Generally, if you want to make a tinfoil read and say that you have one, make sure to have at least one really good reason! For example, here, you could say that Bob was spamming the thread (like is ISO) but not very constructively, which would make your tinfoil a lot more solid!

Or, they are joking around in the thread, but aren’t really talking about important stuff, like their reads, or what they think about other people, or who they scumread. This is good for a couple of reasons: a) it’s better for town if people have productive discussions so if someone’s not doing that, what’s their reason? And b), this is a very basic type of read, but mafia don’t like posting, or watch their words more, and end up “lurking” in the sidelines. A good example is rayford.

There are a couple of ways to defend yourself. Most of the time, it’s just refuting the arguments against yourself. But your argument against snoozing was that he was not active; if he saw your argument and decided to be active, does that mean he’s town? He would do that as scum too, so why is that towny? Is defending yourself towny or scummy? There’s also the fact that there’s no definite “proof” in mafia because it’s a fancy guessing game! So sometimes, it’s smart to think twice before you scumread someone because they did or didn’t react to your scumread on them.
